narration: Half of Americans claim Social Security the moment they turn 62. That decision alone could cost them $182,000 — and most never see it coming.
visual: Person silhouette with a dollar sign on the chest, a bold AGE 62 badge below them, and the hook text animating in from above on dark background

narration: Claim at 62 and you get around $1,400 a month. Wait until 70 and that becomes $2,480 — a 77% permanent pay raise just for waiting eight years.
visual: Two animated bar charts growing side by side on light background: shorter red bar CLAIM AT 62 with $1,400/mo counter, taller green bar WAIT TO 70 with $2,480/mo. '+77% PAY RAISE FOR LIFE' badge pops in.

narration: The break-even point — where waiting actually wins — hits around age 79. And the average 62-year-old is expected to live to 84.
visual: Timeline ruler from age 62 to 85 on dark background. Red fill line grows across it; green circle appears at age 79 labeled BREAK-EVEN. Stat box: Average life expectancy: 84.

narration: From 79 to 85 that's five extra years of $2,480 instead of $1,400. Every year, the gap compounds — and most people have no idea how fast it adds up.
visual: Year-by-year horizontal bar chart ages 79–85 on light background. Each row: short red bar $16,800/yr vs long green bar $29,760/yr, sliding in one at a time.

narration: Run the math all the way to 85 and early claimers leave $182,000 on the table. One impatient decision — six figures gone.
visual: Piggy bank SVG filling up in red as a counter ticks toward $182,000 on dark background. Big number zooms in below: 'left on the table by claiming eight years too early'.

narration: If your health is decent, delay to 70 — it's the closest thing to free money your retirement will ever see. Follow for more wealth math.
visual: White card on light background with three numbered steps sliding in: 1) Delay to age 70, 2) Grow savings 8 extra years, 3) Lock in $182K more lifetime income. CTA text fades in below.
